metadata.title:
WHAT PRISONERS GET


At THE COUNTY JAIL

pages.raw_text:
A signed statement has been is
sued by Allen Custer, member of
the steam shoveler's union. who by
grace of the police spent two
months or more in the county Jail,
when he was guilty of no crime.


Custer takes the treatment of
prisoners within the walls of the
county pail severely, to task, espe-
cially the meals served there at an
cents per day. His conclusions back
up the statements heretofore made
in The Press that there seems to be
combine between the police and
the prosecuting attorneys office to
keep the county Jail full, in order
to swell the board bills being drawn
by the sheriff.


THE food served to the prisoners
In the county Jail is very meager,
said Custer, "of inferior quality and
could be served by any caterer I'M
der like circumstances for not to
exceed 10 cents per day.


WHAT THEY GET.





daily, one in the morning and the
other atnoon. For breakfast the
prisoners are given a pint of much,
wish little piece of sugar about
the size of quarter; small chunk
of bread and cup of very poor
quality coffee, without milk or other
trimmings.


''At noon the meal consists of po-
tatoes, chunk of boiling beef not
much larger than a walnut. a quar-
ter of loaf of bread and something
resembling a dash of gravy, all
served in a tin pan. A little of the
same thin coffee served at break
fast is again dished out. The men
are expected to save enough of this
already scant portion to serve them
for supper.


THE prisoners do the cooking
